title: Intelligence Briefing Dashboard
subtitle: Weekly Conflict Monitor · All Regions
sources: Reuters · AP News · Al Jazeera · ACLED · US Embassy Alerts · LiveUAMap · BNO News · ISW · British Defence Intelligence · Kyiv Post · Sudan Tribune · Irrawaddy · CNN · NBC · CBS · Fox News · HRW · UN News · CFR · Times of Israel · NPR · CNBC · Atlantic Council · Sudan Tribune · US State Dept · OHCHR · Wikipedia · Washington Post

regions.name: Middle East
overview: The US-Iran war escalated dramatically rather than de-escalating: after the 7 Jul tanker strikes and initial US retaliation, Iran launched two further waves of missiles/drones at Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar (9 Jul) and then UAE, Oman (11-12 Jul), while the US conducted a third strike wave (~140 targets) after Iran attacked a third tanker and declared the Strait of Hormuz closed — a widening regional war now touching eight countries.

Gaza's ceasefire remained hollow with near-daily lethal Israeli strikes (1,072+ killed since the truce) even as Hamas dissolved its civil administration in favor of a UN-backed technocratic committee (NCAG) that Israel has not yet allowed to enter Gaza, creating a fresh governance-vacuum risk.

Syria suffered a second mass-casualty Damascus bombing in five days (2 Jul, 7 Jul during Macron's state visit) — authorities announced arrests of an ISIS-affiliated cell — while Israeli settler encroachment into the Golan/Hermon buffer zone extended into a reported new settlement inside Daraa governorate.

Israel faces compounding pressure from multiple directions simultaneously: a disrupted Iranian espionage/terror plot, intensifying West Bank settler violence (20+ incidents in one weekend, journalists attacked), and direct exposure as a likely target if the Iran war widens further.

countries.name: Gaza
incidents: 1 Jul — Gaza-wide: Israeli fire killed 3 Palestinians despite the ceasefire.
5 Jul — Khan Younis/al-Mawasi/Gaza City: Israeli drone strikes killed 6, including a vehicle strike on al-Rashid Street and a tent strike in al-Mawasi.

6-7 Jul — Gaza City/Cairo: Hamas dissolved its 20-year civil administration, transferring authority to a UN-backed technocratic National Committee (NCAG) headquartered in Cairo; Israel has not yet allowed it to enter Gaza.
8 Jul — Gaza Strip: Israeli fire killed 9 Palestinians, per medical sources, the latest in a string of ceasefire violations.
countries.coa: The ceasefire remains formally intact but continues hollowing out — cumulative deaths since the truce began have reached 1,072+ per Gaza's Health Ministry — while Hamas's dissolution of its civil administration in favor of the UN-backed NCAG marks real political movement, undercut by Israel's refusal so far to let the new authority physically enter Gaza. CRITICAL maintained; watch whether NCAG gains actual access, and whether the daily strike tempo starts to fall.

countries.name: Lebanon
incidents: 6 Jul — Nabatieh al-Fawqa: Israeli drone strike on a vehicle killed at least 4, including a school principal, her mother, a domestic worker and a Syrian national.
5-6 Jul — Southern Lebanon: Continued intermittent Israeli strikes on alleged Hezbollah sites during the Khamenei mourning period.

9-12 Jul — regional spillover risk: The widening US-Iran war, now touching eight countries, raised renewed risk that Hezbollah could be drawn into a broader Iran-aligned exchange, though no major Hezbollah-initiated attack was confirmed this week.
countries.coa: Unchanged — continued lethal Israeli strikes despite the formal truce, and a US-Iran war that has now spread to eight countries keeps alive the risk Hezbollah is pulled off the sidelines. CRITICAL maintained.

countries.name: Iran
incidents: 7 Jul — Strait of Hormuz: Iran struck a Qatari LNG tanker and a Saudi tanker; the US responded with strikes on 80-90+ Iranian targets and reimposed oil sanctions.
9 Jul — Jordan/Gulf: IRGC fired ballistic missiles at Jordan's Al-Azraq air base and a US command center, and launched drones at Kuwait, Qatar and Bahrain.

11-12 Jul — Gulf-wide: Iran attacked a third tanker and declared the Strait of Hormuz closed; the US conducted a third strike wave (~140 targets); Iran fired missiles/drones simultaneously at the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman and Bahrain, wounding civilians in Qatar and Kuwait.
8-9 Jul — Tehran/Najaf/Karbala: Khamenei was buried at Iran's holiest shrine after a funeral drawing 15 million+ mourners.
countries.coa: This is now open, widening warfare rather than a collapsed ceasefire — three rounds of US strikes and Iranian retaliation have touched eight countries in five days. CRITICAL maintained; watch for a reported new negotiation track (possibly Switzerland) against the alternative of further geographic expansion, and monitor post-Khamenei succession dynamics as a wildcard on Tehran's posture.

countries.name: Yemen
incidents: 3 Jul — Sanaa: Houthis claimed to confront Saudi warplanes over alleged airspace violations, threatening strikes on Saudi airports and 'vital interests.'
5 Jul — Jabal Dabbas, Hodeidah: Houthi forces attacked government positions with mortars, drones and snipers in some of the fiercest clashes in years; 16 government troops killed, 22 wounded.
5-8 Jul — off Hodeidah: An armed skiff fired on a cargo ship ~30nm southwest of the city; crew reported safe.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained — the fiercest Houthi-government clashes in years, an unresolved Red Sea shipping threat, and explicit Houthi threats against Saudi Arabia all coincide with a widening Iran-US war that raises the risk Yemen becomes a secondary front, including a possible resumption of Houthi attacks on shipping in solidarity with Tehran.

countries.name: Israel
incidents: 6 Jul — Israel-Syria border: IDF detained Israeli civilians attempting to cross into Syria amid reports of a new Israeli settlement established inside Syria's Daraa governorate.
Ongoing — Israel: Authorities disrupted an alleged Iranian espionage and terror plot, arresting a 22-year-old accused of planning explosives production and targeting senior officials.
11-12 Jul — West Bank-wide: At least 20 settler-violence incidents over one weekend, including an armed attack on journalists near Sinjil; 4 settlers arrested.
9-12 Jul — nationwide: Defense Minister Katz put the IDF on alert for renewed confrontation with Iran as the US-Iran war widened to strike the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman and Bahrain.
countries.coa: Upgraded to CRITICAL. Israel now faces compounding risk from multiple directions at once: explicit alert posture against a widening Iran war that has already reached six other countries, a disrupted Iranian espionage/terror plot on home soil, intensifying West Bank settler violence, and a new unauthorized settlement push into Syrian territory. No confirmed direct Iranian strike on Israeli soil this week, but the trajectory across all fronts is deteriorating.

countries.name: Iraq
incidents: 8 Jul — Baghdad: A senior energy-sector executive was arrested as part of a sweeping anti-corruption campaign, amid wider governance strain.
Ongoing — Al-Anbar/Baghdad: Open-source risk trackers flagged both provinces as watch-priority zones citing military dissent and sectarian rhetoric.

9-12 Jul — regional spillover risk: With the US-Iran war now touching eight countries, Iran-aligned militias retain elevated potential to resume attacks on US positions in Iraq, though no new confirmed strike landed this week.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No confirmed militia attack landed inside Iraq this week — though reporting for this window was comparatively thin — but the further widening of the US-Iran war keeps elevated the risk that Iran-aligned PMF factions resume attacks on US facilities (Baghdad Embassy, Ain al-Asad, Erbil) in solidarity with Tehran.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
incidents: 3 Jul — Sanaa/Saudi airspace: Houthis claimed to have confronted Saudi warplanes, threatening strikes on Saudi airports over alleged airspace violations.
7 Jul — Riyadh: Saudi Arabia condemned the Damascus bombing that struck during Macron's state visit.
9 Jul — Riyadh: Saudi Arabia condemned Iran's missile and drone attacks on Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No confirmed attack landed on Saudi soil this week — notably, Saudi Arabia was not among the countries hit in Iran's 9 Jul or 11-12 Jul retaliation waves, unlike Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, the UAE and Oman. Explicit Houthi threats against Saudi airports keep risk elevated; watch closely whether Saudi Arabia is drawn into the expanding circle of direct Iranian retaliation targets.

countries.name: Syria
incidents: 2 Jul — Damascus (al-Marjah): A bomb at a cafe near the Palace of Justice killed at least 9-10 and wounded 22; no group claimed responsibility at the time.
7 Jul — Damascus (near the Four Seasons Hotel): Twin IEDs detonated during French President Macron's state visit, killing 1 and wounding 36; Macron was unharmed.
9 Jul — Damascus: Authorities announced the arrest of an ISIS-affiliated cell said to be behind both bombings.
6 Jul — Daraa/Mount Hermon: Reports emerged of a new Israeli settlement established inside Syrian territory, with the IDF detaining Israeli civilians attempting further crossings.
countries.coa: Upgraded to CRITICAL. Two mass-casualty Damascus bombings within five days — one during a head-of-state visit — plus an active ISIS-affiliated cell (now reportedly arrested, though the claim requires verification) and continued unauthorized Israeli settlement expansion into Syrian territory together represent a serious deterioration in the capital's security and the state's territorial integrity.

countries.name: Egypt
incidents: 7 Jul — Cairo: President Sisi inaugurated a new Strategic Command HQ showcasing counter-terrorism and electronic-warfare capabilities.
Ongoing — Cairo: Egypt's Air Defense Forces began deploying domestic encryption systems to harden military communications.
9 Jul — Cairo: A presidential decree appointed a new chairman of the National Authority for Crisis and Emergency Management.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No dated kinetic incident on Egyptian soil this week; this week's moves (counter-terror showcase, communications hardening, emergency-management leadership change) read as preparatory posturing for potential regional spillover from the widening Iran war rather than a response to any direct incident.

countries.name: Jordan
incidents: 9 Jul — Al-Azraq air base: Iran's IRGC fired 10 ballistic missiles at the base and a US command center; Jordan intercepted 8, with no confirmed casualties or major damage.
Ongoing — Jordan has intercepted 261 of 281 Iranian projectiles aimed at the country since late February 2026, underscoring it is a recurring target.
11-12 Jul — Jordan was notably not among the countries hit in Iran's latest retaliation wave against the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman and Bahrain.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. The successful interception with zero casualties on 9 Jul is a positive data point, but Jordan's role as a recurring Iranian target and continued US basing at Al-Azraq keep it at elevated risk; any future unintercepted strike would justify an upgrade to CRITICAL.

countries.name: Libya
incidents: 2 Jul — Location unspecified: A fire at a security-headquarters facility; cause remains under investigation.
2 Jul — Chad-Libya border region: Two abduction/hostage incidents involving Chadian nationals, consistent with established cross-border criminal patterns.
4 Jul — Tripoli: PM Dbeibah convened a security meeting with newly appointed intelligence chief Abdulmajid Meliqta.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No confirmed major attacks this week; monitor the new intelligence-service leadership for signs of internal power struggles, and continue tracking the Chad-Libya border for cross-border criminal activity.

countries.name: Tunisia
incidents: 8 Jul — Tunis: A court sentenced 21 people, including Ennahda party leaders and former officials, to 12-35 years on terrorism/state-security charges; exiled former Ennahda president Rached Ghannouchi received 14 years in absentia.
Ongoing — Southern Tunisia: Continued spillover from Libyan instability, including cross-border weapons trafficking.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. The 8 Jul mass sentencing of Ennahda figures is a notable political-stability risk factor — such verdicts have historically triggered protest activity in Tunisia — worth watching closely in the coming weeks alongside the ongoing southern-border spillover from Libya.

regions.name: Ukraine/Russia
overview: Kyiv absorbed its deadliest strikes of the year in back-to-back waves (1-2 Jul, 27-31 killed; 6 Jul, 22+ killed; 11 Jul, further casualties), even as Ukraine's own deep-strike campaign expanded into a systematic refinery-targeting operation reaching Omsk, Yaroslavl, Saratov and Syzran — hitting Russia's fuel infrastructure roughly every 1-2 days.

Russia's fuel crisis has become a nationwide emergency spanning 78 of 83 regions (~50 million people), with diesel prices in Moscow up 43%, rationing to 20L/vehicle in places, and emergency imports now arriving from India, Belarus, Kazakhstan and China.

Kostiantynivka remains contested rather than fallen — Russia's MoD repeated its capture claim to Putin, but Ukraine's General Staff continues to deny it amid roughly 255 daily combat clashes concentrated on the Pokrovsk-Kostiantynivka axis.

Moldova's government-formation crisis deepened with a second PM nominee in a week (investor Vasile Tofan, 11 Jul) facing a 15-day confirmation clock and an outright Socialist Party boycott, while Georgia's protest movement passed its 590th consecutive day under a still-tightening detention law.

countries.name: Ukraine
incidents: 1-2 Jul — Kyiv: An 11-hour Russian missile/drone barrage hit every district; a 9-story building partially collapsed in Darnytskyi, killing 27-31 in the deadliest Kyiv strike this year.
6 Jul — Kyiv (nationwide): Russia fired 351 drones and 68 missiles (29 ballistic) overnight, killing at least 22, timed on the eve of the NATO Ankara summit.

6-7 Jul — Omsk/Yaroslavl, Russia: Ukraine struck Russia's largest refinery at Omsk (2,500km, deepest strike of the war) and the Slavneft-Yanos refinery at Yaroslavl (700km), halting production at both.

9-12 Jul — nationwide: Russian strikes killed at least 10 more (9-10 Jul) and hit Kyiv, Kharkiv and Odesa again (11 Jul, 2 killed in Odesa); Ukraine's HUR struck the Syzran refinery in Samara region (12 Jul).
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, deepening. Kyiv has now suffered its two deadliest strikes of the year within a single week, air-defense strain is growing amid reported Patriot-interceptor shortages, and Kostiantynivka remains genuinely contested. Ukraine's refinery campaign has evolved into a systematic, near-daily operation (Omsk, Yaroslavl, Saratov, Syzran) that is measurably degrading Russian fuel output faster than Moscow can offset it.

countries.name: Russia
incidents: 2 Jul — nationwide: The fuel crisis spread to 78 of 83 regions (~50 million people); June crude throughput was ~28% below the 5-year average.
6-7 Jul — Moscow region: 430+ Ukrainian drones targeted Moscow overnight, with all four major airports imposing flight restrictions — one of the largest drone raids on the capital of the war.
6-12 Jul — Omsk/Saratov/Syzran: A rolling campaign of Ukrainian strikes disabled or halted processing at four of Russia's largest refineries within six days.

Ongoing — nationwide: 38 regions imposed fuel-sales restrictions, Moscow diesel prices rose 43%, and Russia began importing gasoline by ship from India and by rail/pipeline from Belarus, Kazakhstan and China.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained; the fuel crisis has crossed from regional to genuinely nationwide, with real civil-unrest potential given rationing and price shocks. Compounded by record-scale drone raids on Moscow itself, this now carries meaningful domestic-stability risk alongside the unchanged external military threat to Ukraine.

countries.name: Belarus
incidents: 2 Jul — Bryansk border area: A Ukrainian drone struck a Minsk-Anapa tourist bus near the Belarus-Russia border, injuring 3 Belarusian nationals.

~29 Jun-3 Jul — Ukraine-Belarus border: Belarus shut down four Russian-installed drone relay stations on its territory after a Ukrainian ultimatum; no further Russian drone transits via Belarus were reported afterward.
6 Jul — Minsk: Lukashenko told military academy graduates Belarus 'will not join the war,' resisting continued Kremlin pressure.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Lukashenko continues actively resisting deeper entanglement — shutting down Russian drone relay infrastructure and publicly ruling out troop deployment — even as rotational mobilization drills and border-infrastructure construction near Ukraine continue. A renewed relay dispute or Belarusian military (not just civilian) casualties would be the key trigger for escalation.

countries.name: Moldova
incidents: 3 Jul — Chișinău: PM Alexandru Munteanu resigned, triggering full government resignation.
8 Jul — Chișinău: Deputy PM Eugeniu Osmochescu was sworn in as acting PM for the transition period.

11 Jul — Chișinău: President Sandu nominated investor Vasile Tofan as PM; he has 15 days to present a cabinet, while the pro-Russian Party of Socialists has declared it will not legitimize the process.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. A second PM nominee within a week (Tofan, an investment-firm partner) now faces a 15-day confirmation clock and an outright opposition boycott. Watch whether Tofan secures parliamentary confirmation by roughly 26 July; failure would raise pressure for snap elections, which the Socialists are explicitly positioning for.

countries.name: Georgia
incidents: Ongoing through 7-10 Jul — Tbilisi: Pro-EU protests continued past their 590th consecutive day outside parliament, still demanding release of political prisoners and a return to the EU accession course.
Ongoing — Tbilisi: Courts continue sentencing protesters to 4-5 days detention under the new automatic-detention law for minor offenses such as standing on sidewalks near demonstrations.
Ongoing — Brussels: EU sanctions debate over Georgia remains stalled due to Hungarian and Slovak vetoes.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. The risk here is chronic and structural rather than a single new flashpoint — a sustained, institutionalized crackdown apparatus (automatic detention, high sanction volumes) continues alongside a protest movement approaching its 600th day, while EU enforcement remains gridlocked.

countries.name: Armenia
incidents: 6 Jul — Yerevan: Law enforcement searched the residence and businesses of opposition Prosperous Armenia Party leader Gagik Tsarukyan amid a fraud/money-laundering investigation.
Ongoing — Yerevan: Russian state-linked hacking operations continue targeting Armenian military, diplomatic and political figures.

9 Jul — Baku/Yerevan: Azerbaijan's FM said border delimitation is largely complete and Baku considers its peace-agreement obligations fulfilled, with Armenia's pending constitutional amendment the remaining sticking point.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No kinetic incidents this window; risk is primarily political (the Tsarukyan investigation could become a domestic flashpoint) and cyber. The peace process is nearing a procedural threshold — Armenia's constitutional-amendment track is the key indicator to watch over the coming months.

countries.name: Azerbaijan
incidents: 5 Jul — Mykolaiv region, Ukraine: A drone strike hit a SOCAR (Azerbaijan state oil company) fuel station in Ukraine, part of a recurring pattern of strikes on SOCAR-owned facilities there.
6 Jul — Baku: Azerbaijan summoned Russia's ambassador to protest the SOCAR facility strike and prior damage to its embassy/consulate facilities in Ukraine.
countries.coa: LOW maintained for Azerbaijani territory itself; exposure remains indirect via continued risk to SOCAR commercial assets and diplomatic facilities inside Ukraine as the wider war intensifies.

regions.name: Sahel
overview: Mali's Anéfis siege turned into what analysts called the largest single offensive of the Mali war since 2012 — JNIM and the Azawad Liberation Front massed roughly 5,000 fighters across five simultaneous fronts on 4 July, ambushing a relief convoy and downing a helicopter, before a large FAMa/Africa Corps counter-column recaptured the town by 9-10 July, though Kidal itself remains beyond government control.

Niger's Tillabéri crisis deepened with a mass-casualty garrison attack at Diaguru (21 soldiers killed, 2 Jul) landing on top of the still-unresolved, still-unacknowledged reports of a Presidential Guard mobilization and armored-unit mutiny in Niamey.

Sudan's El Obeid has not fallen but a UN Fact-Finding Mission this week found the same 'distinct markers of genocide' documented at El Fasher are now being actively repeated there, as RSF forces seized an aid convoy and tightened the siege to all but the city's eastern approach.

Mauritania saw a sharp and unexpected escalation — post-election protests in Bogué, Kaédi, Nouadhibou and Nouakchott turned violent, three protesters died in or shortly after custody, over 200 were arrested, and mobile internet was cut nationwide for days, a materially different risk profile than the country's prior quiet baseline.

countries.name: Mali
incidents: 4 Jul — Aguelhok, Anéfis, Gao, Sévaré, Kéniéroba: JNIM and the Azawad Liberation Front launched a coordinated five-front dawn offensive involving roughly 5,000 fighters; a prison near Bamako was attacked at Kéniéroba.
5 Jul — Anéfis: A relief convoy from Gao was ambushed near Tabrichat, the road mined and a Mi-24 helicopter shot down, as JNIM-FLA besieged the Malian/Africa Corps garrison.

8-10 Jul — Anéfis: A large FAMa/Africa Corps relief column fought through and recaptured the town after heavy losses on both sides; Kidal, roughly 100km away, remains under JNIM-FLA control since falling in April.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Anéfis was recaptured, but analysts called the 4 July offensive the largest since the 2012 rebellion — JNIM-FLA demonstrated the ability to mass thousands of fighters, down a helicopter and rout a relief convoy. This is not de-escalation; the Gao-Kidal corridor remains highly contested and Kidal itself remains lost.

countries.name: Niger
incidents: 2 Jul — Diaguru, Tillabéri: JNIM/ISGS-aligned militants overran the army barracks, killing 21 soldiers, destroying vehicles and seizing weapons — the latest in a near-monthly pattern of mass-casualty garrison attacks.

3-4 Jul — Niamey: Reports, still unconfirmed by the government, described an armored unit refusing deployment to frontline Tillabéri positions (Inatès, Chinagodrar); the Presidential Guard reinforced the palace with armored vehicles overnight.
countries.coa: Upgraded to CRITICAL. The Diaguru attack (21 killed) continues a near-monthly drumbeat of 50-70+ casualty garrison raids in Tillabéri, while the unresolved, still-unacknowledged mutiny/mobilization reporting in Niamey signals genuine internal strain that the junta will not address publicly — a compounding rather than resolving risk. Treat continued government silence on the mutiny as itself a negative indicator.

countries.name: Burkina Faso
incidents: 4-5 Jul — Di, near Dédougou: Suspected JNIM fighters killed roughly 14 soldiers and 7 civilian militia (VDP) in an attack on a military base.

6 Jul — Djibo: A New Humanitarian investigation found Burkinabè army units actively preventing civilians from leaving jihadist-besieged towns, reportedly to obscure the true scale of displacement — corroborating and extending last week's findings.
5-6 Jul — Solhan/Séguénéga: JNIM claimed further attacks on army positions; exact casualty figures remain undisclosed amid the continuing information blackout.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The Di/Dédougou toll (~21 dead) plus on-record testimony that the army is trapping civilians inside besieged towns like Djibo both point to deterioration, not stabilization. Reporting remains a near-total blackout — treat the absence of state reporting as denied access, not calm.

countries.name: Sudan
incidents: 6 Jul — El Obeid: RSF now controls all routes into the city except the eastern approach; roughly 500,000 residents remain trapped, the city has been without power for nearly a month, and food prices are up 300%.

8 Jul — El Obeid: A UN Fact-Finding Mission found the same 'distinct markers of genocide' documented at El Fasher are now being actively repeated in El Obeid; fighting cut power to the western part of the city.
9-11 Jul — El Obeid: The UAE pledged $30 million in emergency humanitarian aid; the RSF seized an aid convoy on 11 July, compromising humanitarian access further.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. El Obeid has not fallen — SAF still holds the city itself — but this week's UN genocide-marker finding applied explicitly to El Obeid, combined with the seized aid convoy and near-total route encirclement, points toward exactly the El Fasher-style atrocity scenario feared last week. Treat humanitarian access as now actively compromised rather than merely constrained.

countries.name: Chad
incidents: Ongoing — Lake Chad basin: UNHCR warned the basin is 'at a tipping point,' with recorded incidents up 80% since January 2024 and roughly 60,000 displaced in Lac province since the May state of emergency.

Ongoing — Tiné, Chad-Sudan border: RSF drone strikes spilling over from the Darfur/El Obeid campaign have intensified since early May; over 90% of Tiné hospital's surgical admissions are now drone-strike casualties.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No new confirmed Lac province ISWAP/Boko Haram incident this week — likely a reporting gap rather than a lull, given the pattern of near-monthly attacks. A second, growing risk vector has emerged in eastern Chad: RSF drone strikes spilling over from the Sudan war around Tiné, distinct in actor and mechanism from the Lac province threat and worth tracking separately.

countries.name: Mauritania
incidents: 29 Jun — Nationwide: Incumbent President Ghazouani was provisionally reported re-elected; the Constitutional Court confirmed his win with 56.12% on 3 July, which opposition candidate Biram Dah Abeid refused to recognize, alleging ballot-stuffing.

1 Jul — Bogué, Kaédi, Nouadhibou, Nouakchott: Post-election protests turned violent; security forces clashed with demonstrators in Kaédi amid looting, and mobile internet was cut nationwide with no government explanation.
2 Jul — Nationwide: The Interior Ministry confirmed 3 protester deaths (two in detention, one in hospital) and over 200 arrests (100 each in Nouakchott and Nouadhibou).
countries.coa: Upgraded to HIGH. Three deaths in or shortly after custody, a sustained multi-city crackdown, over 200 arrests, and a multi-day unexplained nationwide internet shutdown represent a materially different risk profile than Mauritania's prior quiet baseline — this is now an internal political-stability event. Recommend downgrading only after full restoration of communications, due-process handling of detainees, and no further protest-related fatalities over 30 days.

countries.name: Nigeria
incidents: 1 Jul — Mairari FOB, Guzamala LGA, Borno: An ISWAP night attack aimed at disrupting resettlement was repelled, killing 2 soldiers and 1 CJTF member.
5 Jul — Talakawa, Chibok LGA: Troops rescued 6 more abductees from the 29 June Lassa school abduction, though dozens of the roughly 36-37 students taken remain unaccounted for.

7-9 Jul — FOB Logomani, Ngala LGA: ISWAP fighters attacked the base, killing one soldier and briefly breaching the perimeter before being repelled; a police outpost, health centre and two classrooms were burned.
10 Jul — Borno-wide: Troops thwarted coordinated ISWAP infiltration attempts at multiple locations around Maiduguri's outskirts overnight.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The Lassa abduction crisis remains open with dozens still missing more than a week after the attack, while ISWAP sustained a multi-axis offensive tempo around Maiduguri (the Logomani base attack, coordinated infiltration attempts) showing undiminished operational capacity. Sustained pressure and hardened school security in Borno remain required.

regions.name: Asia-Pacific
overview: China ran a broadening, multi-theater grey-zone campaign this week — its first-ever open-ocean submarine-launched ballistic missile test (6 Jul, ~7,200km into the Pacific), simultaneous 'Joint Sea-2026' drills with Russia off Qingdao, a renewed Senkaku coast guard standoff with Japan, and a 50+ vessel swarm near Philippine reefs — signaling a broader coercive posture than any single flashpoint.

Pakistan's crisis widened beyond the Balochistan insurgency and Afghan border war into a serious new internal front: sustained lethal unrest in Pakistan-administered Kashmir after security forces opened fire on mass protests, plus the first confirmed Afghan drone strikes onto Pakistani soil.

Myanmar's junta is now fighting on three fronts simultaneously — Rakhine (where the Arakan Army holds 89% of the state and is besieging the Kyaukphyu SEZ and Sittwe), Ayeyarwady, and Sagaing — stretching its capacity as the AA's 2027 'final victory' deadline approaches.

North Korea continued DMZ fortification and destroyer weapons trials while explicitly condemning deepening South Korea-Japan defense cooperation, and India recorded its most tactically sophisticated Naga insurgent ambush in years in Manipur, tied to a June internal insurgent-group split.

countries.name: North Korea
incidents: 3 Jul — Sea of Japan/West Sea: North Korea conducted a cruise-missile test from its new destroyer Kang Kon, observed by Kim Jong Un; the vessel is to be commissioned within two months.
9 Jul — Pyongyang: KCNA condemned expanding South Korea-Japan military logistics cooperation as a 'foolish act courting self-destruction.'
Ongoing — DMZ: Fortification continues faster than South Korean planners expected, with fencing now within roughly 80m of the Military Demarcation Line.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. No new kinetic provocation this week, but sustained DMZ militarization, destroyer weapons trials and rhetoric against Seoul-Tokyo cooperation show continued military build-up with no de-escalation signal.

countries.name: Myanmar
incidents: 1 Jul — Kyetmauk Taung/Maungdaw, Rakhine: Myanmar Air Force jets bombed border villages, wounding at least 3 civilians including 2 children and destroying homes.

Ongoing — Kyaukphyu: The Arakan Army continued encircling the Taung Maw Gyi naval station defending the China-backed SEZ; the junta is using land, sea and air forces in fierce but unsuccessful counterattacks.
Ongoing — Sittwe: AA forces pressed toward the Shwe Min Gan naval base, with fighting reported within 2km of the Regional Operations Command.
Ongoing — Ayeyarwady Region: The junta launched a major counter-offensive to retake the Pathein-Monywa highway, diverting resources away from the Rakhine front.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The Arakan Army now holds roughly 89% of Rakhine State, with only Sittwe and parts of Kyaukphyu and Manaung still contested — the junta is fighting simultaneously in Rakhine, Ayeyarwady and Sagaing, stretching its capacity. Kyaukphyu's fall would be a strategic inflection point given direct Chinese economic interests there.

countries.name: Pakistan
incidents: 1 Jul — Balochistan: Afghan Taliban forces fired drones into Pakistani territory for the first time in this cycle, injuring two near a government school.

4-8 Jul — Balochistan (Ziarat, Lasbela): Coordinated BLA/TTP-linked attacks killed 42, including a checkpoint raid where 18 abducted police were later executed and a highway ambush on a military convoy.

3-9 Jul — Rawalakot/Muzaffarabad/Mirpur, Pakistan-administered Kashmir: Mass protests (up to 80,000 in Rawalakot) turned lethal after Rangers/police opened fire; reported deaths at Neelum Bridge and Amb village, with 600+ activists arrested — a significant new internal crisis not previously tracked.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, trending worse. Beyond the Balochistan insurgency surge and the first confirmed Afghan drone strikes onto Pakistani soil, a serious new internal front has opened in Pakistan-administered Kashmir with sustained lethal unrest — a genuinely new crisis vector with direct India-Pakistan friction implications given India's response of increased LoC surveillance.

countries.name: India
incidents: 6 Jul — Ukhrul district, Manipur: Two Assam Rifles personnel were killed in a two-hour ambush near Nungshang Kong Bridge by a suspected Naga armed faction; NSCN-IM denies involvement, pointing to a Myanmar-based splinter group.
Ongoing — Jammu & Kashmir: India intensified Line of Control surveillance in response to unrest in Pakistan-administered Kashmir, coinciding with the Amarnath Yatra pilgrimage security period.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, with rising risk flagged. The Manipur ambush is more tactically sophisticated than recent Naga insurgent activity, suggesting a fracturing, radicalizing insurgent landscape, while unrest in Pakistan-administered Kashmir raises spillover pressure along the LoC that warrants close monitoring.

countries.name: China
incidents: 6 Jul — South Pacific: China conducted its first-ever open-ocean submarine-launched ballistic missile test, firing roughly 7,200km into the Pacific; the US, Japan, Australia and New Zealand formally objected.
6-13 Jul — Yellow Sea: China and Russia began 'Joint Sea-2026' naval exercises off Qingdao, including submarine search-and-rescue and air/missile defense drills.
7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: Japanese and Chinese coast guards faced off again over a Japanese fishing vessel, the second such confrontation since 10 June.
7 Jul — West Philippine Sea: 50+ Chinese vessels swarmed Iroquois Reef and Sabina Shoal.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, trending toward the top of the band. The combination of a first-ever Pacific SLBM test, simultaneous joint drills with Russia, a repeat Senkaku confrontation, and record-level grey-zone pressure at Scarborough Shoal (933 ship-days in H1 2026) represents a broadening, multi-theater coercion campaign rather than a single flashpoint.

countries.name: Taiwan
incidents: 3 Jul — ADIZ: Taiwan detected 30 PLA sorties, 26 crossing the median line.
4 Jul — ADIZ: 8 PLA sorties detected, 6 entering the southwestern ADIZ.
6 Jul — ADIZ: 5 PLA sorties detected, coinciding with China's SLBM test and Joint Sea-2026 launch.
Ongoing — Taiwan ran interdepartmental drills to practice securing supply chains and escorting commercial vessels through a simulated PRC maritime blockade.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Sortie volumes have eased toward a pre-2024 baseline, but the concurrent SLBM test and Taiwan's own blockade-rehearsal drills signal both sides increasingly treating a blockade/quarantine scenario as plausible.

countries.name: Philippines
incidents: 7 Jul — Iroquois Reef/Sabina Shoal: 50+ Chinese vessels swarmed the area in the West Philippine Sea.
Ongoing — Scarborough Shoal: China Coast Guard presence remains at record levels (933 ship-days in H1 2026, nearly matching all of 2025).
11 Jul — Manila: The Philippines marked the 10th anniversary of the 2016 South China Sea arbitral ruling; fishermen report access to Scarborough Shoal has worsened, not improved, over the decade.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No kinetic escalation this week, but grey-zone pressure continues at or near record levels with no resolution in sight via the stalled Code of Conduct negotiations track ahead of the Philippines' ASEAN chairmanship.

countries.name: South Korea
incidents: 9 Jul — Seoul: North Korea condemned a new South Korea-Japan military logistics cooperation agreement as 'self-destructive.'
6-13 Jul — Yellow Sea: China-Russia 'Joint Sea-2026' drills near Qingdao remained within South Korea's monitoring range.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, driven primarily by North Korea's fortification activity and rhetoric rather than any new South Korea-specific incident this week. Continued Seoul-Tokyo defense integration is likely to keep drawing pointed North Korean reactions.

countries.name: Vietnam
incidents: Ongoing — Spratly Islands: Vietnam continued rapid reclamation and construction, adding roughly 534 acres over the past year per satellite tracking, raising friction potential with both China and the Philippines.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No new dated kinetic incident this week; underlying construction-driven friction with China and the Philippines persists as a structural risk to monitor rather than an acute one.

countries.name: Japan
incidents: 7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: Japanese and Chinese coast guards faced off over a Japanese fishing vessel, the second such confrontation since 10 June.
8 Jul — Tokyo: Japan and NATO officials voiced shared concern over China's 6 July long-range missile (SLBM) test.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, watch for upgrade. Senkaku confrontations are becoming a near-monthly pattern, and Japan-China relations remain in a declared diplomatic crisis since PM Takaichi's November 2025 Taiwan remarks.

countries.name: Indonesia
incidents: 2 Jul — Ipdeheik Airstrip, Yahukimo, Papua: TPNPB-OPM fighters attacked and torched a humanitarian aid aircraft, killing American pilot Nikolas Gosselin; the investigation into the killing continued through this week.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No new escalatory incident beyond the already-reported pilot killing, but the case remains open; monitor for further TPNPB-OPM attacks on civil aviation and humanitarian access in the Papuan highlands, and for US diplomatic follow-up.

countries.name: Thailand
incidents: 6-15 Jul — Sattahip: CARAT Thailand 2026, a multinational exercise with the US and Canada, got underway.
1 Jul — Chanthaburi: Thailand hosted ASEAN observers at the Thai-Cambodia border fence project, reported over 45% complete.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. The Thailand-Cambodia border ceasefire (in effect since 27 December 2025) continues to hold; this week's activity was routine defense cooperation rather than conflict-related.

countries.name: Bangladesh
incidents: 6 Jul — Savar, near Dhaka: Attackers threw petrol bombs at an opposition (National Citizen Party) rally, wounding four activists.
Ongoing — Nationwide: Rights group ASK recorded at least 66 politically-related killings and 61 deaths in police custody in the six months since the current government took office.
countries.coa: LOW maintained, trending upward. A persistent, low-grade drumbeat of political violence — rally attacks, mob violence, custodial deaths — keeps Bangladesh close to a MODERATE threshold even without a single dramatic trigger this week.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
incidents: 8 Jul — Negombo: The government opened a formal criminal investigation and appointed a 3-member commission, including a retired Supreme Court judge, into the prior week's prison riot that killed 26-28.
10 Jul — Nationwide: The government announced measures to address chronic prison overcrowding (Negombo was built for ~900 but held nearly 2,400 at the time of the riot).
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No new incident this week, but the formal investigation and overcrowding-reform response show government engagement with root causes; monitor implementation and unrest risk at other overcrowded facilities during any inmate-transfer process.

regions.name: Americas
overview: Venezuela's constitutional vacuum moved from looming to active — Delcy Rodríguez's 180-day interim mandate expired 5 July with total institutional silence, unions and civil society marched on SEBIN headquarters demanding elections, and a follow-on nationwide protest is planned for 28 July, the two-year mark since the disputed 2024 election.

Colombia's presidential-transition crisis showed early signs of rhetorical de-escalation — Petro pledged a 'peaceful transition' on 10 July even while the formal transition team remained suspended and Petro simultaneously called for protests on 20 July — while the ELN sustained 53 documented actions against security forces in Chocó over six days.

Cuba's grid suffered a second total national collapse within five days (6 and 10 July), the fourth of 2026 and worst acceleration yet, triggering renewed cacerolazo protests and record monthly protest counts (107 in June).

Haiti's Gang Suppression Force gained a sixth contributing nation (Sri Lanka, 10 July) but remains under 1,000 of its planned 5,500 personnel even as gang violence displaced nearly 6,000 people from Kenscoff in a single week, with the UN Security Council's next review due 14 July.

countries.name: Venezuela
incidents: 5 Jul — Caracas: Delcy Rodríguez's constitutional 180-day interim-presidency mandate expired with no election called and no successor mechanism activated; legal analysts declared the government now in 'unprecedented unconstitutionality.'
6 Jul — Caracas: Rodríguez alleged opposition 'destabilization' attempts exploiting the earthquake emergency, using the disaster to justify continued rule without an election timeline.
8 Jul — Caracas: Unions and civil society marched to SEBIN headquarters demanding elections and the release of political prisoners; a follow-on nationwide protest was announced for 28 July.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The constitutional vacuum flagged previously has now fully materialized — Rodríguez governs past her legal mandate with no judicial or legislative correction — while earthquake-recovery grievances (death toll past 3,500, 63% disapproval of the response) fuse with political demands ahead of the 28 July protest anniversary. Risk of a security-force crackdown rises as that date approaches.

countries.name: Haiti
incidents: 4-9 Jul — Kenscoff, West Department: Viv Ansanm-affiliated gunmen attacked repeatedly, spreading violence across multiple neighborhoods; at least 8 killed and roughly 5,840 people displaced.
10 Jul — Port-au-Prince: Sri Lanka formally joined the Gang Suppression Force, becoming its sixth contributing nation, though the force remains under 1,000 of its planned 5,500 personnel.
11 Jul — Port-au-Prince: Haiti's electoral council cleared 316 political parties ahead of long-delayed elections, a rare institutional bright spot.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The GSF remains critically understrength despite Sri Lanka's accession, while gangs continue actively expanding territory in the West Department. The 14 July UN Security Council briefing is the key near-term event to watch for any acceleration in troop pledges or funding.

countries.name: Colombia
incidents: 1-6 Jul — Chocó: The ELN carried out 53 violent actions in six days tied to its founding anniversary, including a drone-borne explosive attack on the Tadó police station.

7 Jul — Bogotá: President-elect De la Espriella formally suspended the presidential transition, accusing outgoing President Petro of attempting a 'coup d'état' after Petro refused to recognize the runoff result.

10 Jul — Bogotá: Petro promised a 'peaceful transition' while simultaneously calling for protests on 20 July, ahead of the 7 August inauguration; EU and Carter Center observers found no evidence of fraud.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, but the institutional crisis shows early signs of rhetorical de-escalation. Petro's pledge of a peaceful handover is a positive signal, though the transition team remained suspended and Petro is simultaneously organizing 20 July protests. Combined with the sustained ELN Chocó offensive, recommend downgrading only if transition work formally resumes before 7 August.

countries.name: Cuba
incidents: 6 Jul — Nationwide: A total collapse of the national electric grid left roughly 10 million people without power, the third total blackout of 2026.
7 Jul — Havana: Renewed cacerolazo protests broke out across multiple neighborhoods.

10 Jul — Nationwide: A second total grid collapse within five days — the fourth of 2026 and seventh since the crisis began in 2024 — again following a Santa Clara-Sancti Spíritus transmission line failure.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, escalating within the band. Two total grid collapses within the same week indicate the grid has lost all redundancy — a materially worse trajectory than the prior single-collapse pattern — while monthly protest counts (107 in June) are the highest since July 2021. The root cause, the US blockade on Venezuelan oil shipments, remains unresolved.

countries.name: Ecuador
incidents: 2 Jul — Washington: The US designated the 'Chone Killers,' a Los Choneros splinter faction, a Foreign Terrorist Organization.
5 Jul — Guayaquil: An armed attack at a nightclub killed 3.
8 Jul — Guayaquil: A security operation killed two suspected Los Choneros members with records for murder and organized crime.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Homicide trajectory remains near record levels; the scale of militarization (75,000 troops deployed, 70% of the population under nightly curfew) and a US visit to assess possible joint-operations use of a former base at Manta are significant developments worth flagging even without a level upgrade.

countries.name: Peru
incidents: 2 Jul — Lima: The government declared a 60-day state of emergency across 796 districts ahead of anticipated 2026-27 El Niño rainfall.
6 Jul — Ayacucho/Cusco/Junín (VRAEM): The counter-narcotics state of emergency was extended for another 60 days.
9 Jul — Lima: President-elect Fujimori said her incoming government will issue emergency decrees on El Niño response; formal credentialing is set for 15 July ahead of the 28 July inauguration.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No new VRAEM combat incident this week, but dual emergency declarations (climate and counter-narcotics) plus an extremely narrow 0.24-point electoral margin create latent instability risk heading into the 15 and 28 July transition dates.

countries.name: Bolivia
incidents: Ongoing — Cochabamba/La Paz/Potosí: Demonstration activity dropped sharply following the 20 June state of emergency, from 200+ events the prior month to just over 40, though some roadblocks persisted.
Ongoing — Nationwide: Blockade-related economic losses in Cochabamba alone have already exceeded all of 2025's losses; cumulative national losses exceed $1.6 billion.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. The state of emergency has suppressed blockade activity for now, but analysts assess the truce as fragile given unresolved fuel shortages and fragmented movement demands; reassess to HIGH if blockade activity resumes at scale.

countries.name: Dominican Republic
incidents: Ongoing — Nationwide: The homicide rate held at 7.05 per 100,000 as of mid-June, the lowest recorded for that period in recent years.
Ongoing — Dajabón border: The high-volume Haitian-migrant deportation campaign continued at a reported pace of roughly 10,000 per week.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. No security incidents rise to briefing-worthy severity this week; continue monitoring the Haiti border deportation campaign as a potential humanitarian/diplomatic flashpoint given Haiti's deteriorating security.

countries.name: Jamaica
incidents: 6 Jul — Nationwide: Murders were down 24% and shootings down 27% year-to-date, the fourth consecutive year of first-half declines.
8-9 Jul — Spanish Town, St Catherine: Police fatally shot two men in an operation tied to a pump-attendant shooting; INDECOM opened an investigation.
11 Jul — Ballard's Valley, St Elizabeth: One man was fatally shot during a targeted anti-crime operation.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. Overall crime continues declining, but flag the rising police-lethality rate (up ~25% year-on-year) as a separate human-rights/oversight watch item; monitor INDECOM's investigations as an indicator of oversight functioning.
